heading: OPERARE IN TUTTE E SU TUTTE ESERCITARE IL POTERE
card_summary: This long section of the Cantico Terzo, Tono Quarto, treats the human capacity for universal operation and prophetic power. Zorzi opens with the Hermetic axiom (Trismegistus) that God made the world and man as two images of himself, then catalogues occult sympathies — magnetic, mineral, animal, and astral — drawing on Pliny, Cicero, Democritus, and Pythagoras. He argues that man, as the vinculum of all things and uniquely suffused with divinity, can operate upon all things once he governs himself. The section develops through a classification of true prophecy: dreams (Matthew, Job, Pharaoh, Nebuchadnezzar), hypnagogic visions (Ambrose, Lucian the priest), and divine furor (Plato's Ion and Phaedrus, Saul's prophetic ecstasy, Caiphas), culminating in a survey of ecstasy from Plotinus, Pythagoras, and Zoroaster through Francis of Assisi, Augustine, Thomas Aquinas, and Mary of Egypt. The Kabbalistic term bat qol ("daughter of the voice") is introduced as the Hebrew name for all forms of reflected divine speech. Sources include Hermes Trismegistus, Cicero's De divinatione, Plato, Aristotle's Meteorologica and Nicomachean Ethics, Proclus, Porphyry, and Ptolemy's Centiloquium.
essay:
# OPERARE IN TUTTE E SU TUTTE ESERCITARE IL POTERE
## (Cantico Terzo, Tono Quarto, pp. 2207–2280)

*Note: This essay represents an expanded analysis of the major themes in this section, which develops across four principal chapters exploring occult sympathy, divine indwelling, prophetic classification, and ecstatic knowledge.*

---

## Foundational Premise: Hermetic Cosmology and the Microcosm-Macrocosm Identity

Zorzi's treatment of human operational power (*operare in tutte*) is grounded in a foundational claim drawn directly from Hermetic theology. He begins not with original argument but with authoritative citation: God the supremely overflowing Artisan ("Artefice sommamente sovrabbondante") created two images of himself — the world and man — in order to delight in one and enjoy every pleasure in the other. This opening invocation of Trismegistus (whether directly from the *Poimandres* or from Renaissance compilations of Hermetic fragments) establishes the metaphysical architecture upon which the entire section rests.

The microcosm-macrocosm doctrine was not original to the Renaissance, but Zorzi's invocation of it serves a particular function: it justifies the claim that human action can be *universal* in scope. If man is truly an image of the cosmos, then the causal nexus that governs all natural things — sympathies, correspondences, influences — extends into human agency. Man is not merely a perceiver of universal order but a participant in it, capable of operating upon external reality through knowledge and virtue aligned with that order.

This is neither magic in the strictly operative sense (commanding demons or manipulating spirits) nor mysticism in the purely contemplative sense. Rather, it is a form of natural magic or high natural philosophy (*magia naturalis*) grounded in understanding and exploiting the hidden sympathies latent in creation. The distinction is crucial because it allows Zorzi to defend his position against the charge of trafficking in illicit arts while maintaining that human knowledge can indeed be efficacious in ways that transcend ordinary causation.

---

## The Doctrine of Occult Sympathy (*Sympathia*)

Zorzi's second chapter unfolds through what he terms *sympatheia* — the Greek term for occult cosmic correspondence. He catalogs a dense series of natural phenomena that resist explanation by the scholastic natural philosophy of his time: the magnet's attraction of iron; amber and balas ruby (ruby spinel) when rubbed drawing straw; the *aetite* stone strengthening unborn children; jasper stopping hemorrhages; the *echeneis* (remora fish) halting ships in motion; coral improving human humor when worn at the neck; the hyacinth stone giving strength to travelers; rhubarb expelling excess bile.

Zorzi's treatment of these phenomena is not merely empirical catalog. He emphasizes, repeatedly, that no natural philosopher has been able to articulate the *cause* (*causa*) of any of these operations. This rhetorical move is significant: it acknowledges the explanatory limitations of contemporary natural philosophy while asserting that the *operations themselves* are undeniable. The unknownness of the cause does not negate the reality of the effect.

> «A tutti è noto che nella calamita è presente una potenza con la quale essa attrae il ferro e, analogamente, l'ambra e il balagio, se riscaldati per sfregamento sono in grado di spostare una pagliuzza. Tuttavia finora nessuno di quelli che riempiono ponderosi volumi con le loro trattazioni dedicate alle proprietà naturali è riuscito a dire da donde venga o che cosa sia.»

This passage performs important philosophical work. It admits ignorance of mechanism while defending the reality of operation, a position that will become crucial when Zorzi moves to human prophecy and action. If nature itself contains hidden operations beyond human comprehension, then it is not unreasonable to posit analogous operations in human beings suffused with divine grace.

Zorzi then expands the register to include animal divination and meteorological prodigies. Roosters know the hours; birds predict rain; dolphins forecast storms. Cicero's account (*De divinatione*) is cited for roosters presaging the Boeotian victory over Sparta. The Spartans received portents of their downfall through a monkey disrupting the oracle's apparatus. The chameleon's liver, when burned atop buildings, produces storms and thunder (a claim attributed to Democritus). The *ligirio* stone (possibly beryl) cancels magical veils obscuring sight.

What concerns Zorzi is the *universality* of these phenomena: they span the mineral kingdom, the vegetable realm, the animal world, and even inanimate omens. They are evidence of a cosmic *concert* or *polyphonic harmony* ("il canto polifonico delle cose") — what the Greeks termed *sympathia*. This is not metaphorical language; it is a technical term in Neoplatonic and Hermetic physics indicating the actual interconnectedness of all created things through hidden virtue.

Zorzi acknowledges skeptical voices—Panaetius and Carneades, followed by Cicero, who note that divination through birds and animal entrails often fails. Yet he contends that the *frequent* verification of these omens, and the fact that certain birds presage from the right (the raven) while others from the left (the jackdaw), demonstrates that the principle, if not always reliable in its applications, is fundamentally sound. Inanimate things too offer presage: darkened stars, trembling earth, and split stones preceded Christ's death. Earthquakes have preceded major wars and political upheavals — the fall of Constantinople, wars threatening Venice, slaughter threatening Bologna, calamities threatening various regions. The *universal truth* (Zorzi invokes Matthew 24:7) testifies that the day of judgment will be announced by great earthquakes and unusual portents everywhere, proving that earthquakes, like winds, are drawn from God's treasures.

What emerges is a vision of a thoroughly *sacralized* nature, where every level of creation — from mineral to star — participates in divine communication. Yet Zorzi himself recognizes that ancient philosophers have struggled to explain the natural cause of earthquakes. Anaxagoras attributed them to the aether; Empedocles to fire; Democritus and Thales Milesius to water; Aristotle, Theophrastus, and Albertus Magnus to wind or underground vapor; Asclepiades to collapse or subsidence. Poseidonius, Metrodorus, Callisthenes, the Fates, Seneca, and others were scattered across diverse theories, never discovering the true cause—which proceeds from the supreme Moderator either by immediate command or through appointed ministers, or through a power infused in things and explained for divination of the slaughters that usually follow earthquakes, as Aristotle himself confesses in the *Meteorologica*.

This passage subtly establishes a crucial epistemological position: natural causes, as understood by pagan and Christian philosophers alike, are ultimately instrumentalized by divine will. God acts *per causas secundas* (through secondary causes) and *per se* (immediately), but in either case, the operation reveals divine intention. The magnet's mysterious pull and the earthquake's terrible motion are both signatures of the divine artisan writing upon creation.

---

## Man as the Perfect Bond: Divine Indwelling and Restored Dominion

The transition from cosmic sympathy to human capacity pivots on a single claim: if all things correspond and act upon each other through hidden virtue, and if man is the *vinculum of all things and embraces them all* ("il vincolo di tutte le cose e tutte le abbraccia"), then man can operate upon all things. But this is not automatic. It requires that man be *properly governed* ("suo debito temperamento") and that he preserve *self-mastery* ("gaudeat, et sibiipsi praesideat").

Zorzi's authorities span pagan and Christian sources with remarkable catholicity. Plato in the *Ion* and *Parmenides*; Hermes Trismegistus in the *Poimandres* (addressed to Tat); the Pythagorean Xystus, who declares "man is the temple of God, God is his guest"; Ovid's *Fasti* ("Est Deus in nobis, sunt et commercia coeli"); Paul's declaration in Philippians 4:13 ("I can do all things through him who strengthens me"); and finally Aristotle in the *Meteorologica* and at the close of the *Nicomachean Ethics*, who asserts that no virtue—natural or moral—exists except through God.

> «Quanto più abbandoniamo questa vita animale e umana, tanto più vivremo la vita degli angeli e di Dio. Uniti a essi e restituiti al nostro destino migliore, noi avremo potere su tutte le cose perché su tutte domineremo.»

<cite>p. 2046</cite>

This passage, attributed to divine teaching, encapsulates Zorzi's anthropology: human greatness is accessed through the abandonment of the merely animal and human life, not through the acquisition of external knowledge or instrumental power. The Neoplatonic ascent (*anabasis*) from the sensible to the intelligible, here Christianized, becomes the path to restored dominion over creation.

Zorzi interprets Genesis 1:28 allegorically, a hermeneutic common in Christian Neoplatonism. "Fructify" means bearing good works; "multiply" and "fill the earth" means flooding even the earthly body with divinity; "dominate fish, birds, and beasts" signifies spiritual mastery over the three elemental realms and the infernal powers contained within them. The Fall introduced concupiscence and degradation—man became enslaved, a creature more wretched than the beasts. Yet through virtue and self-victory, man can be restored to prelapsarian dignity and recover that dominion written of in Psalm 8:6: "Thou hast put all things under his feet."

However, Zorzi clarifies that this dominion must be acquired in the *order* established by the supreme Moderator from the beginning. The Genesis command unfolds in stages: first, inner fructification through good works; second, the multiplication and filling of the earth understood as the saturation of the earthly body with divinity through the mortification of animal nature and its elevation toward spiritual nature; finally, the dominion over fish (aqueous and terrestrial virtues), birds (aerial and celestial hosts), and beasts. Since these creatures inhabit three elements, they testify that all other things are subject to human dominion. Yet at a deeper level, birds of heaven can be interpreted as all heavenly hosts (intelligences), fish and beasts of earth as aqueous and terrestrial virtues, and all these as including infernal powers. Thus, retaining God and his grace, filled with divinity through good works, and restored to the original grade, man can easily acquire dominion over all things.

This is neither pantheism nor a claim of human divinity in the Pelagian sense. Rather, it is a sophisticated articulation of what later scholastic theology termed *theosis* or deification: the human person, through grace and virtue, becomes a living instrument of divine action. The indwelling of God ("Deus omnia operans") is not abolition of human will but its perfection and elevation. Man's self-governance becomes increasingly transparent to divine governance.

---

## The Classification and Origins of True Prophecy

Zorzi's third chapter shifts to the specific modality of human power: prophecy. Prophecy is defined as the capacity to *prognosticate the future* ("futura prognosticetur"), which Zorzi identifies as a prerogative of God. He cites Isaiah 41:23 ("Annunciate nobis quae ventura sunt, et dicemus, quia dii estis vos"—"Tell us what is to come that we may know you are gods") and 2 Peter 1:21 ("Not ever was prophecy brought by human will, but being borne along by the Holy Spirit, the holy men of God spoke").

Zorzi then assembles pagan testimony to the reality and necessity of prophecy. The Stoics, as reported by Cicero in *De divinatione* I, held that foresight of the future belongs to the gods alone because there is no determined truth about future contingents in the judgment of mortals. Ptolemy's *Centiloquium* affirms that only those inspired by heaven can predict the particulars of future events. Cratippus, Zeno, and the early Academy, as well as Pythagoras and the Stoics after him, all attest to the reality of many true seers and genuine premonitions. Calanus the Indian predicted Alexander's death; the Persian Magi foresaw that Alexander himself would be the destruction of Asia; Socrates predicted Crito's death within three days; Diotima (the priestess consulted by Socrates in Plato's *Symposium*) predicted plague; Anaximander warned Sparta of an imminent earthquake, urging them to abandon the city—and the mountain Taygetus was nearly torn from the earth like the stern of a ship; Epimenides the divine Cretan, warned by oracle, arrived at Athens ten years before the Persian Wars and performed certain sacrifices, and subsequently declared to the fearful Athenians that the Persians would not come for a decade and, once they did, would depart without success.

Zorzi acknowledges that there are multiple origins of apparent divination. Some arise from craft and experience in managing various affairs—Solon foresaw tyranny in Athens; Cato's prescient mind warned against Caesar: "Beware the boy with the ill-fastened belt." Others are drawn from nature and natural signs: Pherecides, Pythagoras's teacher, observing water drawn from a perpetual well, predicted an earthquake; Thales of Miletus, to shame his detractors and show what a philosopher could accomplish practically, purchased all the olive groves in Milesia before they bloomed, having inferred by learned conjecture that the olive crop would be abundant—which proved true, and he gained wealth, not for its own sake but to shame the envy of his detractors. Physicians, farmers, and sailors, each in their art, presage much from probable signs that we pass over as commonplace.

Zorzi then surveys the forms of legitimate divination in antiquity. The Athenians always employed divine priests (*Mantes*) in public councils. The Spartans assigned an augur to their kings and required his participation in public deliberation; for greater matters they consulted the oracle at Delphi, that of Ammon, or that at Dodona. The Romans, in difficult circumstances, ordered the decemvirs to consult the Sibylline books. The Hebrew kings were accustomed to employ prophets (of whom there were many) or, if they themselves were prophets, to consult God directly, as we read of David repeatedly.

These diviners were of multiple kinds: astronomers (*astronomi*) who observed the aspects of heaven; augurs (*augures*) who watched birds moved by celestial influx or by those who preside over the heavens; haruspices or examiners of entrails (*extispices*) who predicted the future by examining animal viscera; pyromancers (*Pyromantici*) who divined by fire; hydromancers (*hydromantici*) by water; geomancers (*Geomantici*) who used points cast by objects thrown at random or by force applied through arm or hand—the ancients would compose these points into celestial figures for divination; necromancers (*Necromantici*), popularly called mages, who summoned unclean intelligences to learn future events from them.

Zorzi distinguishes sharply between two types of necromancy: those "absolutely ignoble" who sacrifice to unclean intelligences, submitting themselves and committing the twofold monstrous crime of idolatry and self-abasement before already-base beings; and others who, by some power (especially the power of divine names), summon and compel such intelligences to reveal what they have learned of secrets from the superior moderators. Both types are often deceived, Zorzi notes, because demons know nothing of future events disposed by the supreme Moderator except insofar as it has been proclaimed and divulged in the celestial court (and indeed beyond it) before the matter is effectuated. Yet demons can sometimes move birds, arrange animal viscera, direct water, fire, and the hand tracing points, and occasionally agitate the human spirit itself. All diviners operate either through the stars around which angels circle, or through inferior instruments guided by unclean spirits.

There are also natural prognosticators who follow nature as guide: those drawing omens from the lines God has placed on human hands (Job 37:7: "that each may know his works") are called chiromancers; those judging from the shape and complexion of the face are called physiognomists. Some, with acute ingenuity and learned experience, read future events, character, and deeds in the palm and face as in a book. Yet esoteric theologians claim to read on the forehead all that pertains to humans—origins, course, and end. Though such knowledge is possible, it is granted to very few. For this reason Christ told the multitude: "Do not judge by appearances" (John 7:24).

Zorzi closes this survey by noting that there are many other divinatory methods derived from inferior causes or signs, but all reduce to the primary genera described. Having dismissed these secondary forms, he turns to *true* prophets (*veri vates*).

---

## The Four Modalities of Genuine Prophecy and the Kabbalistic *Bat Qol*

Zorzi's fourth and final chapter provides a systematic classification of true prophecy, defined as that which is legitimate and veridical, always proceeding from God and the favor of superior beings. It manifests in multiple forms: through dreams, through that vision occurring between sleep and wakefulness, through furor and spirit-agitation. All these modes converge in one genus of divination that the Hebrews call *bat qol*—"daughter of the voice"—which Zorzi renders as *echo*, that is, a reflection of the voice. These forms are reflections of divine and prophetic speech.

> «Tutti questi modi sono riconducibili a un solo genere di divinazione che gli ebrei chiamano בת קול, bat qol, ossia 'figlia della voce', mentre noi chiamiamo il fenomeno 'eco', cioè una riflessione della voce.»

<cite>p. 2054 / fol. XLIr</cite>

The introduction of the Kabbalistic term *bat qol* is remarkable. In classical rabbinic literature, the *bat qol* ("daughter of the voice") designated a heavenly voice heard in post-prophetic times, a weakened form of prophecy suitable to an age when direct revelation had ceased. Zorzi's deployment of the term here reveals his deep engagement with Jewish mysticism and his attempt to synthesize Hebraic categories with Christian and Neoplatonic frameworks. By subsuming all four modalities under the single Kabbalistic umbrella term, Zorzi asserts a unified principle: true prophecy, however it manifests, is fundamentally a reflection or echo of divine speech, neither direct divine utterance nor demonic deception but a mediated communication aligned with the logos.

**Dreams (*per somnium*):** Matthew recounts the angel appearing to Joseph in dreams, instructing him regarding his wife and the child. Pharaoh dreamed of abundance and coming famine (interpreted by Joseph); Nebuchadnezzar dreamed of the succession of kingdoms (interpreted by Daniel). Both were unworthy to know the mysteries shown them, despite wearing the royal insignia. Job speaks of nocturnal visions in 4:13ff., describing how a spirit approaches in the night and hair stands on end at the voice—illustrating the nature of dream vision.

**Hypnagogic Vision (*inter somnum et vigiliam*):** Ambrose describes his own vision of Gervasius and Protasius: "When the Lord granted me to share in the communion of those fasting and praying, sleep seized me such that I felt neither fully awake nor completely asleep, yet with eyes open I saw two young ephebic youths in whitest garments, the short tunic and mantle." He identifies them as Gervasius and Protasius, who suffered martyrdom for Christ. Similarly, the priest Lucian witnessed a vision in which Gamaliel appeared, indicating the burial place of his body, of his sons, and of the blessed Stephen the protomartyr. Zorzi notes that while the manuscript claims Lucian was fully awake, he suspects a scribal error, since the order of vision requires that it occur to one lying in bed, either sleeping or in dormiveglia. If such a phenomenon occurs in full wakefulness, it is during prayer or in some disposition better than lying down when sleep is about to overtake the senses.

**Divine Furor (*per furorem*):** Democritus asserts that some, seized by furor, sang things neither nature nor art had taught them. Plato notes that he valued no one with great ingenuity unless seized by some furor. Plato recalls Timnichus of Chalcis, who had composed no memorable poem, but once seized by furor, composed the most beautiful hymn to Apollo, which appears divine rather than human. The *Phaedrus* teaches that the doors of poetry are struck in vain without furor. Zorzi adopts Plato's definition: "Furor is abstraction, alienation, and nexus" (*abstractio, alienatio, et nexus*). It is abstraction and alienation from the shadows and reflected images by which bodily senses are excited and deceived; it is a nexus because those becoming alien to animal humanity and adhering to the divine receive from it many things they could never obtain by ingenuity or strength.

Biblical exemplars abound. Saul: "The Spirit of the Lord seized Saul and he prophesied in their midst, transformed into another man, numbered among the prophets, while the people marveled and exclaimed: 'Is Saul also among the prophets?'" (1 Samuel 10:10). Caiaphas, the high priest for that year, prophesied (John 11:51). Many poets, whether filled with love of study, virtue, or some other good reason known to God, seized by furor, could reveal hidden mysteries and even prophesy.

Cicero's *De divinatione* I addresses this precisely: "There is another genus of divination in those who do not proceed by reason or conjecture based on observed and noted signs, but by some excitement of mind, a free and loosened motion of the spirit—this occurs often in sleep and sometimes to those prophesying in furor, as Bacchus to the Boeotians, Epimenides to the Coans, and the Erythraean Sibyl to the Trojans."

> «Inest namque animi praesagatio extrinsecus iniecta, atque inclusa divinitus. Ea si exarsit acrius, furor appellatur, cum a corpore abstractus animus, divino furore concitatur, ut Democritus et Plato asserunt.»

<cite>p. 2058–2059 (Cicero quoted by Zorzi)</cite>

Zorzi notes (correctly) that Cicero errs regarding the Sibyl, whose divinatory genus corresponds to neither dream nor furor—in the Sibyls there is a particular influx of which he cannot speak at present, a mysterious category that Zorzi leaves deliberately veiled.

**Ecstasy and Visionary Elevation:** To divine furor succeeds *excessus mentis* (excess of mind) and *extasis*. Porphyry records that Plotinus was often released from the body, and his face changed when contemplating wonderful doctrines he would later write. Porphyry claims to have witnessed this miracle four times; Plotinus himself, at age sixty-eight, asserts he experienced such vision once while contemplating. Years before, Heraclitus and Democritus likewise did so, which is why they delighted in solitude. Pythagoras remained hidden ten years; Zoroaster twenty. Hermotimus of Clazomenae's soul customarily wandered, leaving the body (as he said) though not actually left. Rabbi Shem Tov ben Falaqera reports that Aristotle in his book *On Bdellium* testified that his own soul, stripped of sensibles, was elevated to contemplation of the highest things. Diomedes of Syracuse, intent on geometric figures, did not perceive the tumultuous destruction of his dying homeland or the enemy at hand. This occurs either from extreme intentness of the speculator or from the strenuous virtues of the man—as Aulus Gellius wrote of the chaste priest Cornelius who, at Padua while Caesar and Pompey conflicted in Thessaly, lifted in mind, foresaw the time, order, and outcome of the battle. Socrates (as Plato writes) from sunrise to sunrise again, without food and without sensation or bodily movement, mouth and eyes fixed on the same spot, cogitating and abstracted...

The Christian parallels are extensive: Francis of Assisi carried outside himself while passing through Borgo San Sepolcro; Augustine absorbed in meditation not noticing a woman consulting him; Thomas Aquinas levitated a cubit from the ground; Mary Magdalene lifted by angels at fixed hours in solitude near Marseilles; Mary of Egypt levitated a cubit in prayer; Clare of Assisi lying from Holy Thursday to Easter Sunday unaware of time's passage. Zorzi adds a personal attestation: "I have verified in another, of name and life similar"—claiming first-hand witness to a comparable case.

**Clear Vision (*per claram visionem*):** Drawing on Proclus, Zorzi concludes by invoking the purified mind that feasts on truth in pure splendor, initiated into "perfect, stable, and sincere phantasms" (*perfecta, stabiliaque et sincera phantasmata*)—not images extracted from the senses but higher archetypes contemplated by prophets. The Plotinian image of polishing oneself into a beautiful statue serves as the precondition: the soul must become a mirror capable of receiving the divine form.

---

## Synthesis and Cosmological Integration

This section represents the apex of Zorzi's anthropological project. Having established that the cosmos is structured through numerical, musical, and angelic hierarchies in the preceding portions of the *De Harmonia Mundi*, he now articulates how man—the microcosmic vinculum uniting all creation—can act within and upon this order. The capacity for prophecy and ecstasy is neither exceptional gift nor mere psychological phenomenon but the natural consequence of man's constitution as image of both God and world.

The integration of multiple authorities—Hermetic, Neoplatonic, Kabbalistic, Islamic, classical pagan, and Christian—under a unified framework exemplifies Zorzi's method: all genuine traditions, properly understood, converge on the fundamental truth of divine indwelling and the restoration of human dominion through ascent, virtue, and alignment with the divine. The *bat qol*, the Platonic *furor*, the Plotinian *excessus*, and the Christian *mystical rapture* are identical phenomena described through the vocabulary of different traditions. Each represents man's recovery of his proper relationship to God and cosmos, a relationship disrupted by sin and concupiscence but recoverable through knowledge, virtue, and grace.

The section does not offer technical instructions for achieving prophetic states. Rather, it charts the metaphysical and theological conditions under which genuine prophecy becomes possible: the mortification of animal nature, the purification of the intellect, the alignment of the will with divine intention, and the restoration of the prelapsarian correspondence between human consciousness and the intelligible order. In this sense, prophecy is not magic but metanoia—the turning around of the entire person toward their true source and end.
